name: Power of a Point
whatIsIt: The POWER OF A POINT theorem links the two pieces a line cut off by a circle. From any point P, if a line meets the circle at A and B, the product PA·PB is the SAME for every line through P. Special cases: two chords crossing inside give PA·PB = PC·PD; from outside, a tangent PT and a secant give PT² = PA·PB. It converts lengths into one neat product equation.
whyNeeded: It turns circle-length problems into simple multiplication equations, and is a staple of Olympiad geometry — especially for finding an unknown segment or proving a tangent length.
realLifeUses: Finding a missing chord length in a circular arch
Computing tangent lengths in gear and pulley layouts
Optics and reflection problems on circular mirrors
Any design where a line crosses a circle in two places

vocabulary.meaning: A segment with both endpoints on the circle.
A line that cuts the circle at two points.
A line touching the circle at exactly one point.

story.title: The Crossing Chords
story.text: Two chords of a circle cross at P. One splits into 3 and 8; the other into 4 and an unknown x. 'Find x,' says Ma'am. Rohan recalls the power of a point: PA·PB = PC·PD, so 3 × 8 = 4 × x. That gives 24 = 4x, so x = 6. 'The two products must match,' Ma'am nods. Rohan grins: 'One multiplication and the missing length appears.'
story.extractedProblem: Two chords cross at P: 3·8 = 4·x. Find x.
story.answerInStory: 6 — because PA·PB = PC·PD gives 24 = 4x, so x = 6.

props.label: Chords cross at P: PA·PB = PC·PD (3·8 = 4·6)

visual.caption: For any point and circle, the product of the two segment lengths along a line is constant. Crossing chords: PA·PB = PC·PD.

standardMethod.summary: Identify the point P and the two segments each line cuts on the circle. Set the products equal: for crossing chords PA·PB = PC·PD; for a tangent and secant from outside PT² = PA·PB. Solve for the unknown length.
steps: Locate the point P and the lines through it meeting the circle.
For two chords through P: PA·PB = PC·PD.
For a tangent PT and secant PAB: PT² = PA·PB.
Substitute known lengths and solve for the unknown.

workedExamples.problem: Two chords cross inside a circle: one is split 3 and 8, the other 4 and x. Find x.
From an outside point, tangent PT = 6 and a secant hits the circle at A then B with PA = 4. Find PB.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the segments instead of multiplying them.
commonMistakes.fix: Power of a point uses the PRODUCT of the two segment lengths, not their sum.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using PT (not PT²) equal to the secant product.
commonMistakes.fix: For a tangent it is PT SQUARED that equals PA·PB (the tangent touches once, so both 'pieces' are PT).

easy.q: Power of a point uses the … of the two segments.

medium.q: Tangent PT = 6, secant PA = 4. Find PB (PT² = PA·PB).

challenge.q: Tangent PT = 10, secant PA = 5. Find PB.

questions.q: For a tangent PT and secant PA·PB, the rule is…
Tangent PT = 8, secant PA = 4. Find PB.
Power of a point combines the segments by…

teachBackPrompt: Draw two chords of a circle crossing at P, split into 3 & 8 and 4 & x. Show a grown-up that PA·PB = PC·PD gives 24 = 4x so x = 6. Then explain the tangent case PT² = PA·PB.

revisionCard.summary: Power of a point: along any line through P, PA·PB is constant. Crossing chords: PA·PB = PC·PD. Tangent + secant from outside: PT² = PA·PB. Multiply the two segments (don't add); for a tangent, square it.

rememberIt.hook: Along any line through P, PA·PB stays the same
rememberIt.unpack: Chords: PA·PB = PC·PD. Tangent: PT² = PA·PB. Multiply, don't add.

studentQuestions.a: It follows from similar triangles formed by the intersecting lines — the equal angles force PA/PC = PD/PB, i.e. PA·PB = PC·PD.

studentQuestions.q: What if P is the centre?
studentQuestions.a: Then every line is a diameter and both segments equal the radius, so the product is r² — the smallest 'power' inside, and it grows as P moves out.
